questions.questionText: Rule of 9's (Wallace) for burn surface area estimation in adults assigns the head and neck:
questions.explanation: Wallace Rule of 9's (adults): Head/neck: 9%, Each upper limb: 9%, Anterior trunk: 18%, Posterior trunk: 18%, Each lower limb: 18%, Perineum/genitalia: 1%. Total: 100%. Modified for children: head is larger proportion (18% in infants — Lund-Browder chart more accurate). Burn depth: superficial (1st degree — epidermis, erythema), partial-thickness (2nd degree — dermis, blisters), full-thickness (3rd degree — all layers, painless). Parkland formula: 4mL × %TBSA × kg (24 hr crystalloid). Reference: Reddy — Burns.

questions.questionText: Joule burn (electrical mark) at the entry site of electrocution shows:
questions.optionB: Pale, raised, cratered appearance with characteristic metallization (copper/aluminum deposition), and 'crocodile skin' pattern
questions.explanation: Electrical burns (Joule burns): at point of contact with conductor. Features: raised, button-like/crater-shaped, pale/yellowish, hard, non-inflammatory. Microscopy: honeycombing of epidermis ('Swiss cheese' pattern), nuclear elongation (streaming). Metallization: metallic particles from conductor deposited in skin. Exit wound: irregular, blasted-out. Lightning: Lichtenberg figures (fern-like/arborescent red marks — pathognomonic, NOT true burns, fade after death). Cause of death in electrocution: ventricular fibrillation (low voltage AC — most common). Reference: Reddy — Electrocution.

questions.questionText: Heat stroke differs from heat exhaustion in that heat stroke involves:
questions.optionB: Core body temperature >40°C (104°F), CNS dysfunction (confusion/coma), and ANHIDROSIS (cessation of sweating — thermoregulatory failure)
questions.explanation: Heat stroke: medical emergency — thermoregulatory failure. Core temperature >40°C (104°F). Classic triad: (1) Hyperthermia, (2) Anhidrosis (dry, hot skin — sweating ceases), (3) CNS dysfunction (confusion, seizures, coma). Mortality: 10-80% if untreated. Treatment: rapid cooling (ice packs, evaporative cooling), IV fluids. Heat exhaustion: heat-related illness WITH sweating (still functioning thermoregulation), core temp usually <40°C, fatigue, weakness, headache, nausea. Can progress to heat stroke. Reference: Reddy — Heat-Related Deaths.
